event: Steina and Woody Vasulka (with Andy Mannik) found The Kitchen in the disused kitchen of the Mercer Arts Center, 240 Mercer Street, Greenwich Village
Operates as artist collective under fiscal sponsorship of Electronic Arts Intermix; co-directors Steina, Woody, Rhys Chatham, Shridhar Bapat, Dimitri Devyatkin, Tom Bowes
Mercer Arts Center building (former Broadway Central Hotel) collapses
Incorporated as 501(c)(3) under name Haleakala Inc (DBA The Kitchen) — meaning house of the sun
Robert Stearns becomes first Executive Director; founding board: Paula Cooper, Caroline Stone, Philip Glass; org reopens at 484 Broome Street SoHo (lease ~$10,000/year)
Arthur Russell becomes Music Director
IRS tax-exempt status effective May 1975 (per ProPublica record)
Robert Longo serves as Acting Video Curator (fall 1977 - spring 1978)
Rhys Chatham returns as Music Director (through 1980)
Mary MacArthur becomes Executive Director; launches The Kitchen Presents (broadcast video for TV) inviting Carlota Schoolman to produce
Dancing In The Kitchen series launched
Filmworks program series launched (Leonardo Katz)
Contemporary Music Series launched; New Music/New York festival founded
New Music America festival established (renamed and expanded from 1979 New Music/New York)
George E Lewis (AACM) becomes Music Director
10th Anniversary; Aluminum Nights celebration
Beastie Boys play one of their early shows at The Kitchen
Move from SoHo to 512 West 19th Street Chelsea (former 1920s ice house)
Inaugural Chelsea programming begins; New Ice Nights
Arto Lindsay (DNA / no-wave) is first Music Director after Chelsea move
The Kitchen purchases its W 19th Street building from Dia Art Foundation
Dance and Process series launched (initially under name Working in The Kitchen) — longest-running named program
Lauren Dyer Amazeen becomes Executive Director
20th Anniversary celebration: The Kitchen Turns Twenty
Dance and Process series formally renamed
Elise Bernhardt becomes Executive Director
TV Dinner series launched (vegetarian buffet + time-based art evening) — runs through 2004
Kitchen House Blend music series begins (runs through 2005)
Debra Singer becomes Executive Director and Chief Curator (first to combine titles)
Tim Griffin announced as next ED and Chief Curator
Tim Griffin formally begins as ED & Chief Curator (40th anniversary year); 40th-anniversary programs include Aluminum Music (Apr 15-16) and Pioneers of the Downtown Sound (Sept 9-10)
Hurricane Sandy hits Chelsea; The Kitchen suffers approximately $450,000 in damages; recovery support arrives from Time Warner, Art Dealers Association, and Andy Warhol Foundation
Getty Research Institute acquires The Kitchen's archive — 5,410 videotapes, 600+ audiotapes, photographs, 246 artist-designed posters covering 1971-1999
FY2014 990 shows program-service revenue spike to $714K (likely the Getty archive accession)
Tim Griffin announces departure from The Kitchen
Legacy Russell announced as next Executive Director and Chief Curator
Legacy Russell formally begins as ED & Chief Curator; 50th anniversary year; first Black ED in The Kitchen's history
The Next 50 capital campaign announced — public goal $40M / $28M-five-year initial framing — for renovation of W 19th Street building (architect: Rice+Lipka)
Building closes for renovation; Without Walls programming period begins at Westbeth Artists Housing (163B Bank Street, West Village) and partner venues citywide
FY2022 990 shows revenue at $4.06M; net assets jump to $11.7M (capital campaign on the books)
Mellon Foundation awards $2M / 36 mo for infrastructure and staffing under Legacy Russell — described as the largest single gift in The Kitchen's history
FY2023 990 shows revenue at $6.74M; net assets at $15.96M (capital campaign continuing)
FY2024 990 shows revenue at $4.32M; net assets $16.64M; second full Without Walls year
Andy Warhol Foundation Multi-year Program Support of $80,000 awarded
Building renovation continues; reopening date not publicly fixed as of this dossier (May 2026); Without Walls programming continues at Westbeth and partners
